Renal interstitial fibrosis.caption: Higher magnification (×400) view of the histological section from the UUO group's left kidney, showing glomerular atrophy, flattened tubular epithelia (indicated as 'f'), cellular casts (indicated as 'g'), and renal tubular vacuolation (indicated as 'h'), as visualized under H&E staining.

Histological section of the left kidney from a rat in the UUO+iohexol group 1 day after contrast administration, revealing extensive renal damage including severe interstitial fibrosis (indicated as 'c'), infiltration of inflammatory cells in the renal interstitium (indicated as 'd'), dilated renal tubules (indicated as 'e'), flattened tubular epithelia (indicated as 'f'), cellular casts in some renal tubules (indicated as 'g'), and renal tubular vacuolation (indicated as 'h'), as observed under H&E staining at ×100 magnification.

All graphs were created using JMP 14.0 software (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC).

The image is obtained using immunofluorescence techniques, demonstrating the regenerative response within the context of necrotic muscle fibers.

This histopathological slide from a muscle biopsy of a patient with a dermatomyositis spectrum disorder shows a perifascicular pattern of myonecrosis, regeneration, and MHC class I expression. The image is obtained using hematoxylin and eosin staining and immunofluorescence techniques, highlighting the characteristic perifascicular distribution of pathology seen in this condition.

This histopathological slide from a muscle biopsy of a patient with a dermatomyositis spectrum disorder shows a perifascicular pattern of myonecrosis, regeneration, and MHC class I expression. The image is obtained using hematoxylin and eosin staining and immunofluorescence techniques, highlighting the characteristic perifascicular distribution of pathology seen in this condition.

This histopathological slide from a muscle biopsy of a patient with immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) shows myonecrosis and regeneration with minimal lymphocytic infiltrates. The image is obtained using hematoxylin and eosin staining and immunofluorescence techniques, illustrating the patchy distribution of MHC class I expression that follows the pattern of necrotic or regenerating fibers.

Spinal meningioma.caption: A 68-year-old female with spinal meningioma, axial CT at T7/8 level demonstrating a spinal meningioma with calcifications (white arrows) and no enhancement, characteristic of meningiomas on CT imaging.

Photograph of a daughter with a 1q21.1 deletion, presenting with intellectual disabilities, strabismus, a pointed chin, and deformed ears, reflecting the variable expressivity and incomplete penetrance of the genetic condition within her family.

A 30-month-old patient with a STXBP6 mutation presenting with dysmorphic features, including a broad nasal root, wide auricles, periorbital fullness, wide mouth, and full lips, as observed in a natural image of the patient's facial features.

Clinical features of a 3-year-and-9-month-old Chinese girl with concurrent MIC-CAP and MWS, presenting images of the hands with short and tapered fingers and clinodactyly of the second toe, observed bilaterally, consistent with the patient’s overall phenotype.

Posterior polar cataract.caption: The MI-OCT image illustrates the depth and width of the trench before dividing the nucleus, with red arrows indicating the measured dimensions. This assessment is crucial during phacoemulsification to prevent capsular rupture and ensure proper surgical technique.

One month postoperatively in a patient who underwent FLACS for a posterior polar cataract, retroillumination reveals posterior stippling on the capsular bag. Despite this finding, visual acuity remains unaffected, indicating that the stippling does not interfere with visual function.

In a patient with a posterior polar catarct undergoing FLACS, a posterior chamber intraocular lens (PC-IOL) is securely implanted within the intact capsular bag. The procedure successfully preserves the posterior capsule, ensuring optimal positioning of the IOL within the natural lens capsule.

Post-nucleus division, the MI-OCT image reveals the posterior capsule (yellow arrow) and the epinuclear plate (yellow bracket). This view is used to confirm the adequacy of the procedure and assess the lens position, contributing to a safer and more effective cataract surgery outcome.

Mandibular prognathia.caption: showing dysmorphic facial features and strabismus associated with severe intellectual disability, hearing impairment, and a striking lack of tear fluid, saliva, and sweat.
The patient exhibits a broad and square lower face, broad chin with mandibular prognathia, wide mouth, and narrow forehead

Diffuse leukoencephalopathy.caption: Figure 14 A patient with longstanding multiple sclerosis with diffuse leukoencephalopathy and a left thalamic demyelinating lesion (a) on FLAIR imaging.

A computed tomography (CT) scan of the brain demonstrating intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) in the right hypothalamus with associated intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) in the basal cisterns, consistent with a Hunt and Hess grade 3 and Fisher grade 4 classification.
Additionally, signs of diffuse leukoencephalopathy with spotty calcifications are observed in both cerebral hemispheres.

Brain magnetic resonance imaging of a patient with CADASIL-like disease demonstrates multiple microbleeds in the bilateral cerebral hemispheres, basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem, and cerebellum, as visualized on susceptibility-weighted imaging or gradient-echo sequences. These microbleeds are characteristic of small vessel disease and contribute to the clinical phenotype of the condition.

Migraine without aura.caption: Fig. 2 Altered effective functional connectivity between the right substantia nigra and whole-brain regions in patients with migraine without aura compared with healthy controls. Blue signifies decreased functional connectivity
Specifically, MwA patients demonstrated significantly greater BOLD responses in the cerebellum relative to both MwoA patients and HC,
while the medial frontal gyrus showed increased activation in MwA patients compared to HC.
These findings suggest distinct neural activation patterns associated with migraine subtypes during THS.

Adducted thumb.caption: This sub-figure shows frontal bossing with a ridge over the metopic suture, hypoplastic supraorbital ridges, and a prominent nasal root, indicative of the characteristic craniofacial features associated with this syndrome.

This sub-figure shows adducted thumbs and tapering fingers with the second and fifth fingers overlapping the third and fourth, reflecting the characteristic hand and digit abnormalities associated with this condition.
This sub-figure presents a high forehead, full cheeks, a broad nasal root, a long philtrum, and micrognathia, which are facial features commonly observed in this syndrome.

Endocardial fibrosis.caption: Repeated cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) of a 72-year-old Japanese man with hyper-eosinophilic syndrome revealing a typical apical thrombus in the left ventricle (LV), characteristic of Löffler endocarditis, with arrowheads indicating the thrombus location.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) of a 72-year-old Japanese man with hyper-eosinophilic syndrome showing a trabecular appearance in the apex of the left ventricle (LV), consistent with Löffler endocarditis, with arrowheads indicating the trabecular structure.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) of a 72-year-old Japanese man with hyper-eosinophilic syndrome demonstrating late gadolinium enhancement of the endocardium in the left ventricle (LV), indicative of endocardial fibrosis, with arrows highlighting the areas of enhancement.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) of a 72-year-old Japanese man with hyper-eosinophilic syndrome displaying late gadolinium enhancement of the endocardium in the left ventricle (LV), consistent with ongoing endocardial fibrosis, with arrows marking the enhanced areas.

Congenital hepatic fibrosis.caption: Figure 2 Numerous liver cysts and signs of portal hypertension with perigastric collaterals (arrow). A magnetic resonance elastography test (at 5.9 kPa) revealed fibrosis. Such findings are suggestive of congenital hepatic fibrosis.

A 32-wk-old female infant with a family history of consanguinity, diagnosed with Calori syndrome (congenital hepatic fibrosis, choledochal cyst type V, and multiple cysts in the kidneys), shows focal intrahepatic dilatation with a central dot sign on abdominal ultrasonography, which is a characteristic finding in choledochal cysts.

A 32-wk-old female infant with a family history of consanguinity, diagnosed with Calori syndrome (congenital hepatic fibrosis, choledochal cyst type V, and multiple cysts in the kidneys), exhibits tubular cystic-like structures within the liver parenchyma on abdominal ultrasonography, consistent with the biliary abnormalities seen in choledochal cyst type V.

A 32-wk-old female infant with a family history of consanguinity, diagnosed with Calori syndrome (congenital hepatic fibrosis, choledochal cyst type V, and multiple cysts in the kidneys), presents with diffusely enlarged liver and coarse parenchymal echogenicity on abdominal ultrasonography, with several round and tubular cystic-like structures visible within the liver parenchyma.

Stress urinary incontinence.caption: A patient with stress urinary incontinence, demonstrating the condition characterized by the inability to maintain urethral closure, leading to involuntary leakage of urine, as seen in the context of a minimally invasive bulking agent therapy approach.
Video S2 Demonstrating loose PUL as cause of SUI. PUL, pubourethral ligament; SUI, stress urinary incontinence.

Light microscope observations of collagen and muscle fibers in the control group demonstrate well-preserved smooth muscle fibers with a gross structural integrity, collagen fibers arranged in tiny bundles, and normal morphology of microvascular cells, as visualized through Masson’s trichrome and H&E staining methods.

Transperineal ultrasound of the same patient with SUI during straining, demonstrating the anatomical changes associated with stress incontinence. The pubourethral ligament (PUL) elongates under effort, and the anterior and posterior vaginal walls (a&p) are stretched backward and downward, opening the posterior wall of the urethra along its length. This mechanical distortion leads to the loss of urethral closure, as described in the pathophysiology of SUI.

Genu recurvatum.caption: This figure illustrates a case of genu recurvatum, where the mechanical axis of the lower limb is anteriorized relative to the joint center, as demonstrated in the radiographic view of the lower limb.

Lateral view of the same 8.5-year-old patient showing macrocephaly, high forehead, flat face, protuberant abdomen, lordosis, tilted pelvis, genu recurvatum, and genu valgus at the left knee, reflecting the characteristic skeletal abnormalities seen in this condition.

Lateral view of the same 8.5-year-old patient showing macrocephaly, high forehead, flat face, protuberant abdomen, lordosis, tilted pelvis, genu recurvatum, and genu valgus at the left knee, reflecting the characteristic skeletal abnormalities seen in this condition.

Lower extremity alignment measurement in the supine position showing genu recurvatum, with a foam roller placed under the ankle and markings indicating the midpoint of the knee joint line, the greater trochanter, and the lateral malleolus.

Macular schisis.caption: OCT images of myopic maculopathy in the ATN classification system, showing no macular schisis. This represents the T0 grade, where the macular structure remains intact without any signs of schisis or detachment.

In the context of high myopia, an axial OCT scan of the posterior pole reveals a full-thickness myopic macular hole with intraretinal cysts in the deep posterior staphyloma, a progressive scleral ectasia that introduces complex tractional forces leading to retinal splitting and cyst formation.

An OCT scan of the posterior pole in a high myopia patient reveals vitreomacular traction with impending macular hole, associated with diffuse macular schisis and foveal detachment, showing outer retinal layer disruption due to traction from a thickened posterior hyaloid undetached from the fovea and deep posterior staphyloma.
The optic disc is marked by double arrows.

Gastric duplication.caption: Laparoscopic resection of gastric duplication, with the use of an electric hook to separate the gastric duplication (GD) from the surrounding gastric wall tissue, demonstrating the surgical technique employed during the procedure.

Laparoscopic resection of gastric duplication, showing blunt dissection using gauze to identify the boundary (marked by a black dotted line) between the cyst and the normal gastric wall muscle layer, highlighting the careful separation technique to preserve surrounding tissue.
Figure 7 Gastric duplication cyst before resection in the operating room (Personal collection).

Laparoscopic resection of gastric duplication, depicting the repair of the gastric wall muscle layer using 5-0 absorption sutures in an intermittent suture pattern, following complete removal of the duplication and verification of no gastric muscle layer perforation with air injection through the gastric tube.

Wide nasal base.caption: A front view of a patient with dysmorphic features including a bitemporal bulbous nose, wide nasal base, and bitemporal narrowing, consistent with a syndrome involving microcephaly, micrognathia, and retrognathia, as well as hypoplastic scrotum and other abnormalities identified in the comprehensive clinical and imaging evaluation.

Exuberant granulation tissue.caption: A histopathological slide of a bronchial lesion in a patient with sarcoidosis, stained with hematoxylin and eosin at ×200 magnification, revealing chronic inflammation associated with exuberant granulation tissue and numerous epithelioid cells, consistent with the findings observed in the prior specimen.

The histopathological slide (HE 100x) reveals exuberant granulation tissue characterized by a proliferation of new blood vessels within a loose extracellular matrix, surrounded by lymphocytes and scattered neutrophils, reflecting the active inflammatory and reparative processes in the middle ear following the extensive infectious infiltration.

Histologic sections of a rectal mass biopsy from a patient with a suspected infectious or neoplastic process, showing colorectal mucosa with ulceration, epithelial cells with nuclear inclusions, exuberant granulation tissue, marked lymphoplasmacytic and eosinophilic infiltrate, and fibrinopurulent exudate, as seen under Hematoxylin & Eosin staining at 400X magnification.

A histopathological slide of a bronchial lesion in a patient with sarcoidosis, stained with hematoxylin and eosin at ×100 magnification, showing non-caseating epithelioid granulomas composed of tightly clustered epithelioid histiocytes, chronic inflammation, exuberant granulation tissue, a large number of multinucleated giant cells, and acellular hyaline substances between the nodules.

Michaelis-Gutmann bodies.caption: Fig. 3 Michaelis-Gutmann bodies are positively stained on von Kossa stain. (A) Hematoxylin and eosin stain. (B) von Kossa stain.

Figure 1. Colonic mucosa with crypt distortion (from ulcerative colitis) and malakoplakia, characterized by diffuse infiltration of the mucosa by eosinophilic histiocytes, some of which contain gray, round calcospherites (Michaelis-Gutmann bodies) (arrowheads), representing undigested bacterial elements associated with calcium and iron deposition. 20× magnification.

The same patient's transverse colon histopathology reveals round basophilic structures, measuring approximately 1 to 10 µm in size, identified within histiocytes; these structures, known as Michaelis-Gutmann bodies, exhibit varied morphologies including laminated, homogeneous, or targetoid appearances with a dense central core, as visualized on histopathological examination.

Left atrial isomerism.caption: Figure 2 Still frame ultrasound images are shown diagnosing heterotaxy/left atrial isomerism. (a) A view of the fetal abdomen is shown with a midline liver; stomach is not shown in this still image but was found to be in normal position on the left side. (b) Cardiac imaging is shown with normal size right and left ventricles. A common atrium is seen. In addition, an azygous vein is seen left and posterior to the descending aorta which is usually seen in left atrial isomerism. Constrictive pericarditis.caption: Echocardiography of a patient with constrictive pericarditis revealing an inferior vena cava dilated (>2.1 cm) with minimal collapse (<50%) with respiration. This finding is indicative of the altered hemodynamics seen in constrictive pericarditis, where the increased ventricular interdependence affects systemic venous return.

Echocardiography of a patient with constrictive pericarditis demonstrating respirophasic interventricular septal motion abnormality, with leftward deviation and flattening of the septum during inspiration (yellow arrow). This finding is characteristic of the increased ventricular interdependence and dissociation of intrathoracic–intracardiac pressures caused by a constricting pericardium.

Fig. 7 Hepatic vein flows obtained in a single patient. A: expiratory diastolic flow reversal characteristic feature in constrictive pericarditis is suspected. B: absence of increase in diastolic flow reversal during expiration. Only prominent A waves are seen in every cardiac cycle. Insp: inspiration, Exp: expiration.

Echocardiography of a patient with constrictive pericarditis showing cylindrical deformity of the left ventricle (yellow arrow), along with right ventricular lateral wall calcification and tethering (green arrow). These features are consistent with the tethering of the LV lateral and right ventricular free walls contributing to the constrictive physiology.

Anterior staphyloma.caption: Figure 1. Facial features. (A) Presence of microcephaly, short broad nose, corrected unilateral cleft lip and palate and short neck. Digital photograph of the right eye of Patient 3, diagnosed with congenital primary aphakia, showing localized corneal opacity with iridocorneal adhesions and the presence of a lens echo as visualized on B scan ultrasonography. These clinical features are consistent with the traditional diagnosis of Peters anomaly, a condition often associated with congenital primary aphakia.

Clinical photographs of Patient 1, a patient with congenital primary aphakia, showing the right eye with a silvery white appearance of the cornea, consistent with corneal opacity, while the left eye exhibits complete anterior staphyloma, a hallmark of congenital primary aphakia. These findings were corroborated by B scan ultrasonography confirming the absence of the crystalline lens.

Clinical photographs of Patient 2, the identical twin of Patient 1, with congenital primary aphakia, showing the right eye with a silvery white appearance of the cornea, consistent with corneal opacity, while the left eye exhibits complete anterior staphyloma, a hallmark of congenital primary aphakia. These findings were corroborated by B scan ultrasonography confirming the absence of the crystalline lens.

Figure 3 USG Doppler study showing Right Renal Vein Thrombosis

Emergency computed tomography of the right iliac fossa graft kidney in a patient with acute transplant-related venous thrombosis (TRVT) and ipsilateral lower-extremity deep vein thrombosis (DVT) reveals a median sagittal section showing thrombosis in the right superficial iliac vein with continuity to the central renal vein, as well as perirenal and adjacent pelvic fatty interstitial exudates and effusions. The thrombosed grafted renal vein is also visualized with a filling defect, confirming occlusion extending from the lower extremity veins to the renal vein.

Laryngeal carcinoma.caption: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of three consecutive sections of laryngeal carcinoma tissue provides histological context, showing the morphological features of the tumor cells for comparison with immunohistochemical findings.

The area of TERT and AP-1 co-expression, visualized as yellow regions in the immunofluorescence image, is delineated within the laryngeal carcinoma tissue, indicating spatial overlap of these proteins in the tumor microenvironment.

Negative control staining of three consecutive sections of laryngeal carcinoma tissue using the anti-APOM antibody shows no specific staining, confirming the specificity of the antibody and ruling out non-specific binding in immunohistochemical analysis.

In human laryngeal carcinoma tissue, quantum-dot based immunofluorescence imaging reveals co-expression of TERT (red) and AP-1 (green) proteins within the epithelial cells of the laryngeal tissue, highlighting their concurrent presence in the neoplastic lesion.

Vocal cord polyp.caption: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of three consecutive sections of laryngeal carcinoma tissue provides histological context, showing the morphological features of the tumor cells for comparison with immunohistochemical findings.

Negative control staining of three consecutive sections of laryngeal carcinoma tissue using the anti-APOM antibody shows no specific staining, confirming the specificity of the antibody and ruling out non-specific binding in immunohistochemical analysis.

Immunohistochemical staining of three consecutive sections of laryngeal carcinoma tissue shows APOM protein expression localized in the nucleus, as detected by the anti-APOM antibody. This finding is consistent with the observed nuclear localization of APOM in laryngeal tissues, which remains to be fully elucidated.

Vacuolated hepatocytes.caption: Photomicrographs of liver sections from BPA-treated groups show distended and hemorrhagic central and portal veins, with loss of normal hepatocyte architecture, degenerated hepatocytes exhibiting pyknotic nuclei, vacuolated hepatocytes, dilated blood sinusoids, and increased Kupffer cell infiltration, as observed under high-power magnification (H&E staining; ×400).

Photomicrographs of liver sections from BPA-treated groups show distended and hemorrhagic central and portal veins, with loss of normal hepatocyte architecture, degenerated hepatocytes exhibiting pyknotic nuclei, vacuolated hepatocytes, dilated blood sinusoids, and increased Kupffer cell infiltration, as observed under high-power magnification (H&E staining; ×400).

Photomicrograph of fetal liver tissue sections obtained on the 20th day of gestation, stained with hematoxylin and eosin, demonstrating a marked loss of lobular architecture. The sections show signs of degeneration, including intracellular vacuolization, ballooning of hepatic cells, damaged hepatocyte regions, and vacuolated hepatocytes, with the central vein exhibiting damaged endothelial lining.

Photomicrograph of fetal liver tissue sections obtained on the 20th day of gestation, stained with hematoxylin and eosin, revealing hepatocyte hypertrophy with a vacuolated appearance and vesiculated nuclei. There is evidence of damaged hepatocyte regions, destruction of liver architecture, and erosion of endothelial cells lining the central vein, along with ballooning degeneration of cells and intracellular vacuolization with prominent nuclei.

Arachnoid granulation.caption: The arachnoid granulation, composed of collagen fibers, connective tissue, and arachnoid cells, is shown in a histopathological slide with elastic fiber staining, revealing scattered elastic fibers within the structure.

In patients with venous sinus stenosis (VS) and idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), axial imaging of the transverse sinus (TS) shows hyperplastic arachnoid granulations with stenosis greater than 66% of the normal sinus diameter.

In patients with venous sinus stenosis (VS) and idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), axial imaging of the transverse sinus (TS) demonstrates hyperplastic arachnoid granulations associated with stenosis greater than 66% of the normal sinus diameter.

A 31-year-old male patient with a 4-year history of left-sided paroxysmal torticollis and mild intermittent headaches, secondary to transverse-sigmoid sinus stenosis (TSS) caused by both intrinsic arachnoid granulation invagination and extrinsic brain parenchymal compression, is shown in a preoperative volume-rendered CT (VRT) image revealing extrinsic compression of the brain parenchyma, which contributes to the TSS, as indicated by black arrows.

Cardiomyocyte degeneration.caption: Histological examination of the left ventricular (LV) myocardium using Masson's blue trichrome staining reveals the normal structure of the myocardium, with well-preserved cardiomyocytes, intact myofibrils, and no signs of degeneration or fibrosis, as seen in the control group.

Discrete multifocal cardiomyocyte degeneration was also observed. This rabbit did not partake the trial period and was inoculated with venom only accompanied by adjuvant (HE, 400X). P.A.S. stain was not positive.

Fig. 2. Histopathological changes in cDMDR hearts. Cardiomyocyte degeneration and
necrosis, mononuclear cell infiltration, and fibrosis are observed in all age
groups. These changes are found diffusely in the ventricular wall and papillary
muscle, and especially prominent in the right ventricular wall and the outer layer
of the left ventricular wall. There are, to some degree, differences in the lesions
between cDMDR of the same age, but histopathological findings are similar for all
cDMDR. There are no apparent histopathological findings indicative of hypertrophic
cardiomyopathy or dilated cardiomyopathy. A, B: Left ventricle of cDMDR at 3 months
of age. Arrows indicate necrosis of cardiomyocytes. C, D: Left ventricle of cDMDR at
5 months of age. Arrows indicate necrosis of cardiomyocytes. E, F: Left ventricle of
cDMDR at 10 months of age. Arrows indicate necrosis of cardiomyocytes. A–F: HE
stain.

Thunderclap headache.caption: Figure 4. Imaging evaluation of a COVID-19 patient with cerebral venous thrombosis. A 59-year-old woman presented with a thunderclap headache followed by a severe progressive headache. Her neurological examination revealed bilateral papilledema. In the brain CT scan, she had an ischemic occipital lesion (panel A). Brain MRV demonstrated lack of flow in the left transverse and sigmoid sinuses, confirming the diagnosis of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (panel B). In addition, the patient reported she had a fever, cough, and sore throat 10 days before her neurological symptoms. Chest CT showed diffuse ground-glass opacification (panel C). A positive SARS-CoV2 PCR confirmed the diagnosis of COVID-19. COVID-19, coronavirus disease 2019; CT, computed tomography; MRV, magnetic resonance venography; PCR, polymerase chain reaction; SARS-CoV-2, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2.

This immunohistochemical analysis, performed using immunoperoxidase reactions with DAB (brown) visualization, reveals the expression of CD11c on these cells, which are integral to the tumor's pathogenesis and osteoclastogenesis.

Immunofluorescence on frozen kidney tissue shows diffuse granular staining of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) for IgG, diagnostic of membranous nephropathy (MN), as seen in this patient with concurrent IgG4-RD and nephrotic syndrome.

Electron microscopy of the kidney biopsy shows numerous subepithelial electron dense deposits associated with diffuse podocyte foot process effacement, with no mesangial or subendothelial deposits, consistent with the ultrastructural findings of membranous nephropathy in this patient with IgG4-related disease.

Empty delta sign.caption: Gradient-echo (GRE) MRI of the brain showed hemosiderin deposits within the cortical veins, which are characteristic of chronic microhemorrhages associated with venous sinus thrombosis and appear as hypointense regions on GRE sequences.

Postcontrast T1-weighted MRI of the brain revealed hyperintense cortical veins, corresponding to the presence of methemoglobin within the thrombus, which typically appears hyperintense on both T1 and T2 sequences after one week of symptom onset.

MRI of the brain with contrast showed the MRI equivalent of the 'empty delta sign' on postcontrast T1 images, demonstrating a central isointense lesion within the venous sinus with surrounding enhancement, indicative of venous sinus thrombosis.

Spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia.caption: Fig 1 Clinical and radiological features of the four index patients with spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia. (A) Patient 1 at 2.6 years. The clinical features include mild coarseness of facial features with short nose, depressed nasal bridge and hypomineralization of the teeth, severe short stature with short neck, trunk, and limbs, narrow thorax, protruding abdomen, and flexion contractures of the hip and knee joints. Pelvis and lower limbs in patient 1 (B,C), patient 2 (D), patient 3 (E,F), and patient 4 (G,H). The iliac bones are short and broad with serrated iliac crests. The acetabulae are flat and wide with spur‐like projections at the medial and lateral margins. The ischia and pubes are thick with serration at the lateral margin of the ischia. The femora are mildly bowed. The metaphyses of the long bones are wide, cupped, and severely irregular (arrows). The capital femoral epiphyses display significantly delayed ossification, while the other epiphyses of the long bones show normal maturation. The epimetaphyseal changes in patient 3 progressively worsened with age (E,F). Megaepiphyses are found at the knee and ankle. Wrist and hand in patient 1 (I), patient 2 (J), and patient 3 (K). Metaphyseal cupping and irregularity of the radius and ulna are apparent in patients 1 and 3, while those are mild in patient 2. Metaphyseal cupping and irregularity of the metacarpals and proximal phalanges are also found in patients 1 and 3. Delayed carpal bone age is detected in all three patients, but it is more severe in patients 1 and 3. No carpal ossifications are visible in patient 3 at 5 years. Brachydactyly is not apparent in all.

Exposure to birds.caption: FIGURE 1 Subject 1: 9-year-old male referred with possible diagnosis of childhood interstitial and diffuse lung disease. He complained of recurrent attacks of dyspnoea and dry cough. He had a strong history of exposure to birds. On presentation, he was tachypnoeic and hypoxic (oxygen saturation 88% at rest in room air). He had marked failure to thrive with first-degree clubbing, and auscultation revealed widespread fine crepitations. Echocardiography showed dilated right side with pulmonary hypertension. a) Axial computed tomography shows bilateral scattered subsegmental consolidative patches associated with few scattered atelectatic bands; b) interlobular and fissural thickening; c) bronchocentric chronic inflammation (low power); d) small poorly formed granuloma (high power) (haematoxylin and eosin c) ×20, d) ×200). Overall, the features are consistent with hypersensitivity pneumonitis. There was a significant clinical improvement on elimination of bird exposure and systemic steroids.

Periapical lucency reflects periapical tooth abscess (arrow) compared to the normal contralateral left tooth (circle).C, Axial face CT bone window image at the mandibular symphysis location. Vagal paraganglioma.caption: Fig. 1 Imaging features of hypoglossal denervation.aAxial T1W sequence in a patient with acute tongue ptosis due to cranio-cervical junction degenerative disease (CCJD) (see Fig. 5) demonstrates posterior displacement of the affected right hemitongue (arrowheads), which remains isointense in signal and bulk.bAxial T2FS sequence in the same patient asademonstrates posterior displacement (arrowheads) and T2 hyperintensity of the affected right hemitongue. Note the sharply marginated signal change along the median raphe (arrows) which distinguishes this from a mass lesion.cAxial contrast-enhanced CT in a 44-year-old patient presenting with neck pain and tongue deviation demonstrates posterior displacement of the left hemitongue (arrowheads) without change in attenuation. A high cervical carotid dissection was identified (not shown).dAxial T1W sequence in a 29-year-old patient with chronic tongue ptosis due to a jugular paraganglioma (circle). The affected right hemitongue demonstrates volume loss and T1 shortening (dashed triangle) consistent with fatty atrophy.eAxial T2FS sequence in the same patient asddemonstrates signal dropout (dashed triangle) within areas of fatty atrophy, but also some areas of high signal attributed to edema. Note how signal changes respect the median raphe (arrows), distinguishing denervation from a mass lesion. In this case, denervation is caused by extrinsic compression by a vagal paraganglioma (circle) in the carotid space, with characteristic T2 hyperintensity and internal flow voids.fAxial post-gadolinium T1FS sequence in the same patient asdandedemonstrates enhancement of the right hemitongue. Note how signal changes respect the median raphe (arrows), distinguishing denervation from a mass lesion. The vagal paraganglioma (circle) enhances markedly.gAxial T1W sequence in a 54-year-old patient with tongue weakness and episodic dizziness demonstrates T1 shortening and volume loss in the right hemitongue respecting the median raphe (arrows) compatible with fatty atrophy. In this case, denervation is caused by extrinsic compression by a T1 hypointense schwannoma (circle).hAxial T2W sequence in the same patient asgdemonstrates T2 prolongation and volume loss in the right hemitongue respecting the median raphe (arrows) compatible with fatty atrophy. The causative schwannoma (circle) is of intermediate T2 signal with a T2 hyperintense cystic component.iAxial CT in a patient with chronic tongue ptosis due to osseous metastasis (not shown) demonstrates posterior displacement (arrowheads), hypoattenuation, and volume loss in the right hemitongue respecting the median raphe (arrows) compatible with fatty atrophy

Telangiectasia macularis eruptiva persta.caption: Figure 2 Less typical presentations of cutaneous mastocytosis (CM) and differential diagnosis. (a) Macular red-brown skin lesions may be few in number, sometimes even fewer than shown in this picture, which may be easily overlooked. This form has been called “occult CM”. (b) Lesions can be less pigmented or not pigmented and red, which, together with a matted telangiectatic appearance, has formerly been called telangiectasia macularis eruptiva perstans (TMEP). (c) Extensive maculopapular CM may lead to widespread, nearly confluent lesions. (d) Such matted telangiectasias are in fact essential telangiectasias and not TMEP, as they are lacking specific mastocytosis criteria. This patient also presents multiple lentigines, another differential diagnosis, when present in numbers and in locations typical for CM.

Figure 1 (a) Slit-lamp photograph of the bullous keratopathy in the right eye; (b) postoperative aspect of the right eye 1 day after penetrating keratoplasty. Congenital cataract is a disorder of lens transparency present at or shortly after birth. It is a major cause of blindness or treatable visual impairment in children. There are many causes of congenital cataracts. These include intrauterine infections, exposure of the pregnant woman to radiation, medication during pregnancy, as well as genetic and metabolic causes. Cataract surgery may be recommended for preverbal children who have dense cataracts, strabismus, or nystagmus. Some studies have suggested that performing cataract surgery within the first four weeks of life may increase the risk of developing aphakic glaucoma. As a result, it is generally advised to delay the surgery until the patient is at least 28 days old [1]. According to research, between 30% and 50% of congenital cataracts are caused by genetic mutations affecting the proteins in the lens structure. Congenital cataracts are often caused by single-gene disorders. Autosomal dominant inheritance is widely considered to be the most common mode of congenital cataracts. Currently, over 34 loci and 18 genes on different chromosomes have been identified to be associated with autosomal dominant congenital cataracts (ADCC) [2]. A 50-year-old female patient was referred from an outpatient clinic for secondary glaucoma, which was refractory to treatment. The patient has a history of congenital cataracts in both eyes, which were removed when she was one year old, and secondary aphakia. The patient has no systemic conditions, does not take any general medication, and has no family history related to ocular pathologies. Although the most common forms of adult cataracts are age-related, the occurrence of congenital cataracts is often associated with other eye disorders [3]. She was diagnosed with amblyopia in the left eye (LE) and nystagmus in both eyes. The patient underwent surgeries for strabismus in both eyes at the age of 3 (RE) and 5 (LE). She was diagnosed with glaucoma following congenital cataract surgery at age 24 and prescribed topical hypotensive medication. After 9 years, at the age of 35, the glaucoma was no longer properly controlled with maximal topical medication. A bilateral Ahmed valve implantation was performed due to the risk of scarring and bleb failure with a trabeculectomy. Glaucoma is the leading cause of irreversible visual loss and the second most common cause of blindness, leading to a huge burden worldwide [4,5]. Congenital glaucoma commonly occurs among communities that encourage consanguinity and may be classified as primary (without any ocular or systemic developmental anomalies) or secondary congenital glaucoma (accompanied by other pathologies) [6,7,8]. It is characterized by retinal progression loss of ganglion cells, which leads to changes in the optic nerve head and visual field defects, affecting visual quality of life [9]. Aphakic glaucoma is the second most common cause of glaucoma in the pediatric population, being classified as a secondary form of open-angle glaucoma [10]. The development of aphakic glaucoma may be multifactorial. Various risk factors have been identified, including age at the time of lensectomy, type of cataract, IOL implantation, primary posterior capsulotomy with anterior vitrectomy, preexisting ocular abnormalities, additional intraocular surgery, and family history of congenital cataract. The pathophysiological mechanisms of aphakic glaucoma can be classified into two groups: angle closure and open angle. It is believed that angle-closure glaucoma may be caused by an intense postoperative inflammatory response, which can result in synechia in the chamber angle or pupillary block. The mechanism underlying open-angle glaucoma is poorly understood and thought to be multifactorial. Chemical and mechanical hypotheses have been proposed [11]. At the age of 48, the patient was referred to a corneal transplant center with a diagnosis of right eye bullous keratopathy (as shown in Figure 1a). In January 2018, a penetrating keratoplasty (PRK) was performed (Figure 1b). Following penetrating keratoplasty (PKP), the risk of developing glaucoma is significant: different studies report an incidence of 9–31% early after surgery and 18–35% within the late postoperative period [12]. The etiology for this disorder is multifactorial; risk factors contributing to ocular hypertension (OHT) and glaucoma after PKP include preexisting glaucoma, combined surgical procedures, corneal perforation, previous PKP, steroid response, suturing technique, and the diameter of the graft. In addition, the change in anterior chamber angle structure is suspected to be related to the incidence of glaucoma after PKP [13]. A clear understanding of the various mechanisms that operate during different time frames following PK is essential to chalk out the appropriate management algorithms [14]. Wherever indicated, prompt therapy should be initiated to lower intraocular pressure and salvage vision [15]. The surgery was performed without intraoperative complications; the tube remained in a good position without needing replacement. The patient was followed closely and, on the second day, postoperatively, was diagnosed with choroidal detachment (Figure 2a). Suprachoroidal hemorrhage is a rare but potentially devastating complication that can occur during or after penetrating keratoplasty [16]. The risk of occurrence is low, between 0.5 and 1%, and one of the predisposing factors is previous eye surgery [17,18]. It is important for surgeons to be aware of the potential risk of suprachoroidal hemorrhage during eye surgeries and to take steps to minimize this risk, such as carefully monitoring intraocular pressure and avoiding excessive manipulation of the eye. Early detection and prompt treatment are crucial in preventing long-term complications. One month after the PKP, the patient presented a sudden decrease in visual acuity in her right eye, and upon examination, she was diagnosed with rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (Figure 2b).

Elevated jugular venous pressure.caption: Figure 19 Posteroanterior chest X-ray of severe pulmonary stenosis: this chest X-ray is of a 31-year-old female who presented with worsening exertional dyspnea over the last two years. Her exam revealed elevated jugular venous pressure with prominent A wave. She was noted to have a right ventricular lift with a grade 4/6 systolic ejection murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border. The chest X-ray is well centered and there is a good inspiration. The cardiothoracic ratio is normal. The pulmonary trunk is enlarged (its right border is outlined by blue dots as it passes over the left bronchus). The left pulmonary artery branch that is indicated by two red arrows is also enlarged. Her echocardiogram revealed severe pulmonary valve stenosis with peak gradient 68 mmHg and mild pulmonary valve regurgitation. She subsequently underwent balloon valvuloplasty with reduction of her peak gradient to 24 mmHg. Her symptoms of dyspnea had resolved a few months post-procedure.

Pulmonary hamartoma.caption: Pulmonary hamartoma in the right upper lobe, as seen on the ADCtotal map, which integrates diffusion data across multiple b-values to provide a comprehensive assessment of the lesion's diffusion properties.

Pulmonary hamartoma in the right upper lobe, as seen on the pure diffusion coefficient (D) map, which quantifies the pure diffusion component of water molecules within the lesion, independent of perfusion effects.

Pulmonary hamartoma in the right upper lobe, as seen on the perfusion fraction (f) map, which represents the proportion of water molecules diffusing within the perfused compartment of the lesion, providing insight into vascular dynamics.

Pulmonary hamartoma in the right upper lobe, as seen on the perfusion-related diffusion coefficient (Dstar) map, which reflects the diffusion characteristics associated with the perfusion component of the lesion, aiding in the differentiation of benign and malignant lung lesions.

Stage 3 chronic kidney disease.caption: Fig. 3 Vascular aging in full spectrum: a complex case of multisystem involvement. This figure features the case of an 87-year-old female with a comprehensive medical history, including asthma, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, atherosclerotic coronary artery disease, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease, who presented with abdominal pain and subsequently experienced transient slurred speech and right facial numbness. CT angiography highlighted calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the bulbs of the bilateral internal carotid arteries (A, arrows), illustrating the widespread impact of systemic atherosclerosis. MRI FLAIR images revealed white matter hyperintensities (B, arrows), suggestive of chronic CSVD, without evidence of acute ischemia, leading to a diagnosis of a transient ischemic attack. This case underscores the multifaceted nature of vascular aging, showcasing how systemic atherosclerotic changes and small vessel disease converge, affecting both cerebral and peripheral vascular health

Peripheral retinal atrophy.caption: FIGURE 1 Images of P1 (left) and P2 (right). P1's right (a) and left (b) eye showed dull reflexes of the macula and normal optic discs. Peripheral retinal atrophy is visible, without typical bone‐spicule pigmentation. Fundus autofluorescence (FAF) images of the right (c) and left (d) eye demonstrated a hypofluorescent ring on the foveal area. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) in right (e) and left (f) eye revealed peripheral retinal thinning with photoreceptor loss. These paracentral abnormalities are characteristic for retinal dystrophy in P1, specifically rod‐cone dystrophy. In P2's right (g) and left (h) eye, the foveal reflex was absent and the macula appeared granularly with a patchy peripheral retina. Optic discs appeared normal. On FAF, a bull's eye maculopathy was seen in the right (i) and left (j) eye, consisting of an increased autofluorescent ring in the posterior pole. Both increased and decreased autofluorescent patches were found. OCT of the right (k) and left (l) eye revealed absence of the IS/OS junction in the extra‐foveal region. These findings in P2 were suggestive for the diagnosis retinitis pigmentosa [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

Figure 4. Cell morphology. In the menisci from multi-organ donors, the cells embedded in the ECM were round and showed a healthy morphology, consistent with viable and metabolically active cells. In the nucleus, the chromatin appeared diffuse with small condensed areas near the nuclear membrane (A and B). In the young patient the chromatin showed small condensed areas (C and D), that were similar to those in the multi-organ donors with the cytoplasm containing small rare vacuoles (v) and autophagic vacuoles (av). In the older patients with traumatic meniscal tear meniscal cells contained nuclear chromatin that was more condensed than in the donor cells, with a specific pattern, called “chondroptosis”. In the cytoplasm abundant vacuoles were present with scarcity of cytoplasmic organelles and swollen and emptied mitochondria (E, F). Autophagic vacuoles, consistent with oxidative stress, were also present in the cytoplasm. In the representative OA patients, large areas of condensed chromatin (G) and large vacuoles within the cytoplasm were observed (H). Scale bars: A) 2 μm; B-H) 1 μm.

Localized plexiform neurofibroma (pNF) in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), illustrated as a benign, infiltrative tumor that develops along multiple nerves or a nerve plexus, typically present at birth or in early childhood and often disfiguring due to its growth pattern.

Axillary freckling in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), characterized by small, darkly pigmented spots typically found in the folds of the skin, such as the armpits, which usually appear around ages 5 to 8 years and are a common diagnostic feature of NF1.

Diffuse plexiform neurofibroma (pNF) in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), demonstrating a widespread, infiltrative tumor growth along multiple nerves or a nerve plexus, which can be highly disfiguring and may exert pressure on surrounding nerves and structures, with potential for malignant transformation into MPNST.

Thin eyebrow.caption: FIGURE 1 Phenotype of patients. Patient 1: (a) The blue arrows show the discreet left lateralized growth. The neck was short. (b) Facial dysmorphology: thin eyebrows, mid‐face hypoplasia, depressed nasal root, anteverted nostrils, short columella, long philtrum, thin upper lip, thick everted lower lip and macroglossia. (c,d) Bilateral ear pits highlighted with the arrows. (e,f) She had clinodactyly of the 5th toes, a low implantation of the right big toe, and overlapping of the 2nd and 3rd right toes. Patient 2: (a) The blue arrows show the discreet right lateralized growth. (b) Facial dysmorphic features: thin eyebrows, long eyelashes, depressed nasal root, bulbous nose, anteverted nostrils, short columella, long philtrum, thin lips and micrognathism. (c) The arrow shows hypertrophy of the right hemi tongue. (d) Umbilical hernia measuring 2.5 cm long axis

Squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva.caption: Fig. 5 55-year-old woman with FIGO stage 4B squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva who presented with a malodorous vulvar mass.aAxial contrast-enhanced CT of the pelvis demonstrates a fungating vulvar mass (arrow).bBulky left external iliac and inguinal lymphadenopathy was present (arrows).cAxial T1-weighted contrast-enhanced anddaxial T2-weighted images show decreased size of the enhancing vulvar mass (solid arrow inc) and external iliac (dotted arrow) and inguinal (solid arrow in d) lymph nodes following radiation treatment
phenotypes: Squamous cell carcinoma of the vulva

Intestinal pseudo-obstruction.caption: A patient with stiff person syndrome presenting with lumbar pain and chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction, demonstrating clinical hyperlordosis with a bloated abdomen, indicative of significant gastrointestinal distension and musculoskeletal involvement.

Histopathologic findings in a child with chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction (CIPO) show hypoganglionosis, characterized by a reduced number of ganglion cells, observed in the intestinal tissue using TUJ1 immunohistochemical staining at ×10 magnification.

Sagittal CT scan of a patient with stiff person syndrome revealing abundant stercoral stasis within the colon, accompanied by hyperlordosis of the lumbar spine, highlighting the gastrointestinal and musculoskeletal manifestations of the condition.

Plain radiograph of the abdomen in a patient with chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction (CIPO), demonstrating non-obstructive ileus findings characterized by dilated loops of small bowel with minimal air-fluid levels, suggesting impaired intestinal motility without mechanical obstruction.

Depressed nasal tip.caption: A 3-year-old patient with Kabuki syndrome, presenting with characteristic facial features including arched eyebrows, epicanthus, ptosis, prominent eyelashes, long palpebral fissures, eversion of the lateral portion of the lower eyelids in both eyes, and a depressed nasal tip, as seen in the external photograph.

Figure 1 Facial features of the patient.At age 3 mo, he had a long palpebral fissure (A), arched and broad eyebrows with the lateral third displaying sparseness (B), long eyelashes (A), but short columella with depressed nasal tip (C), wide nasal bridge, and high-arched palate as well as a high forehead and hairline (D).

Sex reversal.caption: Scanning electron micrograph of stage 14 embryonic genitalia from a ZZ male Pogona vitticeps incubated at 36°C that did not undergo sex reversal, showing bilobed hemipenes with a sulcus spermaticus. This specimen was histologically validated to have testes, confirming its male sex despite the high incubation temperature.
Figure 1 Complete sex reversal induced bySox9knockdown

Allantoic cyst.caption: Figure 1 We present a case of a 25-year-old G3P1, fit and healthy, who presented to our unit in her first trimester for the combined screening test assessment [1,2]. The early ultrasound assessment of the fetal anatomy demonstrated a cystic structure at the level of fetal cord insertion that was connected to fetal bladder, measuring 21.1 × 23.1 mm (A). The suspicion of allantoic cyst [3] was raised due to the patent urachus demonstrated on the ultrasound assessment [4]. An additional umbilical cyst was seen next to the allantoic cyst. Differential diagnosis with umbilical cord cyst, omphalocele and bladder exstrophy has been considered [5]. No other obvious major abnormalities have been seen and the nuchal translucency was within normal limits with a low chance for common trisomies (T21/T13/T18) from the combined screening test. A follow up scan at 16 weeks showed similar findings with the previous scan, the cystic structure keeping smaller measurements (14.7 × 15.1 mm) (B). The anomaly scan at 19 weeks demonstrated no other structural anomalies beside the cystic structure that was measuring smaller than before (11.1 × 14.5 mm) (C). In view of this findings, the mother chose to have invasive testing (amniocentesis), and the result of microarray showed 1.82 Mb microdeletion in the 1q21.1q21.2 region. The consequences of 1q21.1 microdeletion may include mild developmental delay, dysmorphic craniofacial features, microcephaly, eye abnormalities, cardiac defects, brain malformations as hydrocephalus and agenesis of the corpus callosum [6,7]. Genitourinary anomalies as vesicoureteral reflux, hydronephrosis, inguinal hernia, and cryptorchidism can be present [8]. Because the clinical spectrum is broad and persons with deletion do not have obvious clinical findings, the family decided to continue the pregnancy [9]. In order to establish the familial or “de novo” origin of this microdeletion, parental testing (microarray/qPCR) is advised [10]. The pregnancy evolved without complications and the third trimester scan showed small measurements of the cystic structure. A live neonate was delivered at 39 weeks by elective caesarean, weighing 3000 g (D). The neonate underwent surgical repair of the abdominal wall in the first weeks of life and no other complications have been reported during postnatal care. We have followed up the child within the first year of life and no dysmorphic craniofacial features, microcephaly, or renal disorders have been reported. In terms of brain development, the baby achieved the expected milestones for the first year of life including rolling over, sitting up, standing, and initially walking.

Segmental fracture.caption: A 45-year-old patient with ipsilateral left scapula, clavicle, and proximal humerus fractures, as confirmed by imaging, is shown in the coronal plane of a 3D-reconstructed CT scan of the left shoulder. The anterior surface of the shoulder reveals the highly comminuted scapular fracture involving the scapular body, segmental fracture of the medial border, infraglenoid fracture of the lateral border, and fracture of the scapular spine at the base of the acromion.

A 45-year-old patient with ipsilateral left scapula, clavicle, and proximal humerus fractures, as confirmed by imaging, is shown in the coronal plane of a 3D-reconstructed CT scan of the left shoulder. The posterior surface of the shoulder reveals the extension of the scapular fractures, including involvement of the scapular body, medial and lateral borders, and the scapular spine, with additional comminuted fractures of the lateral third of the clavicle and extra-articular proximal humerus.

Gottron sign.caption: Figure 1 (A) Diffuse hyperpigmentation of face including peri-ocular (heliotrope rash), zygomatic, and nasolabial folds. (B) Hyperpigmented plaque over upper back (Shawl sign). (C) Hyperpigmented papules and macules over the knuckles (Gottron papule and Gottron sign). (D) Hyperpigmented papules over radial aspect of index finger (mechanic’s hand). (E) Dermoscopy of heliotrope rash shows brown reticular network, gray dots and globules (blue arrows) and linear out-of-focus vessels (blue stars) (DermLite DL4, polarized, ×10). (F) Dermoscopy of Gottron papule shows white-to-pink structureless areas (blue arrows) and a reticular pigment network (blue star) (DermLite DL4, polarized, ×10). (G) Histopathological examination shows an atrophic epidermis, basal cell vacuolation, melanophages, perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate and the presence of focal mucin (hematoxylin and eosin, ×200). PAS stain shows the presence of mucin (inset).

Testicular pain.caption: Longitudinal ultrasound images show enlarged, heterogeneously hypoechoic right testis (RT) compared to the normal left testis (LT), indicating abnormal testicular parenchymal echogenicity and possible inflammation or ischemia.

Longitudinal ultrasound images show enlarged, heterogeneously hypoechoic right testis (RT) compared to the normal left testis (LT), indicating abnormal testicular parenchymal echogenicity and possible inflammation or ischemia.

Transverse color Doppler ultrasound (CDUS) image of the scrotum shows normal vascular flow in the left testis, but absent vascular flow in the right testis, suggesting compromised blood supply due to either torsion or severe inflammatory vasculitis.

Fig. 6 Globular testicular enlargement and heterogeneous echotexture.aGray-scale transverse US image of the left scrotum in a 13-year-old boy with 3 days of intermittent left testicular pain shows globular shape of left testis with horizontal lie (arrow), decreased flow and redundant spermatic cord (neither shown), and reactive hydrocele (star). He was found to have a 180° twist on the left with abnormal epididymal attachment (the epididymis was attached only to the superior pole, suggesting bell clapper deformity) and he underwent bilateral orchiopexy after left detorsion.bGray-scale longitudinal US image of the right testis in a 16-year-old boy with acute right testicular pain shows heterogeneous echotexture of the right testis; decreased flow and redundant spermatic cord were also present (not shown). Flow returned after detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy was performed. Intermittent torsion was diagnosed intraoperatively

Short humerus.caption: FIGURE 1 Characteristics of patient. Short humerus, dysmorphic facial features including a large forehead, round face, hypertelorism, depressed nasal bridge with short nose and a long philtrum, cleft lip, and anteverted Nares. The height of patient was normal (A), limited extension in left elbow (B), short first metacarpal on right hand radiography (C), Bilateral radial head dislocation and proximal radioulnar joint disatasis in chest X‐ray (D). Normal hip radiography (E). The C and E radiographies are for when the patient was 4‐year‐old

Gastroesophageal reflux.caption: A slurried barium bolus has just entered the stomach, with a second bolus present within the mid-thoracic esophagus, as seen in the dynamic fluoroscopic view capturing the progression of bolus transit and early signs of potential esophageal dysfunction.

A slurried barium bolus has just entered the stomach, with a second bolus present within the mid-thoracic esophagus, as seen in the dynamic fluoroscopic view capturing the progression of bolus transit and early signs of potential esophageal dysfunction.
Figure 1 Gastrografin swallow study showing gastric outlet obstruction with mild gastroesophageal reflux as seen on fluoroscopy.

Subsequently, gastric content is refluxed into the distal esophagus, as visualized in the fluoroscopic sequence, highlighting the occurrence of GER during or shortly after bolus entry into the stomach, a commonly observed feature in dogs with presumed esophageal dysmotility.

Granular casts.caption: the presence of numerous muddy brown granular casts with bile staining, consistent with obstructive jaundice and biliary obstruction

Figure 5 Pathologic findings of acute tubulointerstitial nephritis (patient 2). Light microscopy: (A) Mixed inflammatory cells infiltrated the tubular epithelium (tubulitis) and interstitium. Large numbers of mononuclear cells, neutrophils, eosinophils, and plasma cells were present (hematoxylin and eosin, ×400). (B) Acute tubular injury with red granular casts and string-like appearing casts was observed(Masson’s trichrome, ×400). (C) The casts show strong myoglobin positivity in the immunohistochemical stain (myoglobin, ×400). Electron microscopy: (D) The tubular casts have electron dense granules (×8000, 80 kv).

Uterine prolapse.caption: Fig. 2 Uterine prolapse and edematous small bowel loops hanging outside the vagina with a view of the dusky appearing mesentery

The red line delineates the posteroinferior margin of the pubis, while the yellow line indicates the pubis–uterine fundus distance at rest, which is used to assess the degree of pelvic organ prolapse (POP) in the midsagittal plane according to established methodology.
Fig. 1 Transvaginal evisceration of the edematous small bowel loops and uterine prolapse at presentation

The red line again delineates the posteroinferior margin of the pubis, while the green line indicates the pubis–uterine fundus distance during Valsalva, allowing for the measurement of changes in pelvic floor dynamics and aiding in the diagnosis of pelvic organ prolapse (POP) based on the difference in pubis–uterine fundus distance at rest and during Valsalva.

Hypoplastic hippocampus.caption: Brain MRI of a 5-month-old individual with TUBA1A variants showing malformations of cortical development, including a simplified gyral pattern and thick cortex with irregular surface and cortex-white matter junction in the perisylvian area, suggestive of polymicrogyria.
The corpus callosum (CC) and brainstem are thin, with disproportionately thick and long mesencephalon and medulla oblongata, respectively.

Bronchiolitis obliterans.caption: shows bilateral pulmonary fibrosis on axial contrast-enhanced CT scan, with signs of bronchiolitis obliterans and focally organizing pneumonia, likely induced by bleomycin, in the absence of clinical pulmonary symptoms.

Computed tomography (CT) scan of the chest in a patient with antibody-negative PAMs, likely triggered by anti-CD20 therapy, showing subtle mosaic attenuation, ground-glass opacities, bronchiectases, and bronchial wall thickening, consistent with a diagnosis of bronchiolitis obliterans (BO). These findings are associated with the Macklin effect and reflect progressive respiratory compromise in the context of severe mucocutaneous involvement and immunosuppression.

Figure 2 HRCT sections of a 45-year-old male with severe shortness of breath. The hyperlucent regions show diminished calibre of vessels and mild segmental and subsegmental bronchial dilatation. The patient was diagnosed to have fixed small airway disease. A clinical diagnosis of bronchiolitis obliterans was made in view of history of chemical fume exposure, progressive disease, and lack of response to glucocorticoids

A 3-month post-operative female patient with progressive dyspnea and no significant past medical history, presenting with hyperinflation of both lungs and diminished vascular shadows on peripheral lung fields on chest computed tomography scan in the inspiratory phase. The imaging modality is a chest CT scan, revealing signs of obstructive lung disease with reduced peripheral vascular markings, but without mosaic perfusion or air trapping, which are typical features of bronchiolitis obliterans.

Liver biopsy of the right hepatic lobe in a patient with necrotic liver mass-related Budd-Chiari syndrome (BCS) and hereditary protein C deficiency revealed hepatic infarction at zone 3 of the hepatic lobule on 4× magnification, showing characteristic necrotic changes in the periphery of the lobule, consistent with ischemic injury.

Liver biopsy of the right hepatic lobe in a patient with necrotic liver mass-related Budd-Chiari syndrome (BCS) and hereditary protein C deficiency revealed hepatic infarction at zone 3 of the hepatic lobule on 4× magnification, showing characteristic necrotic changes in the periphery of the lobule, consistent with ischemic injury.

Small placenta.caption: A 35-week pregnant woman with a history of cesarean section and diagnosed with placenta previa with high suspicion of anterior abnormal invasion to the urinary bladder at 20 weeks gestation, transabdominal ultrasound examination 27 weeks after delivery revealing a small placenta on the anterior uterine wall with a linear endometrium, indicating near-complete placental evacuation and normal menstrual function.

Distal joint hypermobility.caption: Figure 2 Examples of common and clinically significant features. Lower limb magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is also shown. (A) Female Family 25 proband. The pregnancy for this infant was complicated by reduced fetal movements and prenatal ultrasound detection of limb contractures. The image shows typical “frog leg” positioning due to marked congenital hypotonia and weakness. Note also the nasogastric feeding tube and reduced palmar creases(inset). This infant also had mild pulmonary stenosis and needed intubation and ventilation for a short period following delivery. (B, C) The older of the 2 affected brothers (Sibling 1) from Family 1 at age 34 years. Both brothers had congenital scoliosis, which progressed during childhood. Spinal surgery was not possible in the pictured brother due to concurrent development of severe respiratory insufficiency. His respiratory deficit was diagnosed at age 13 years following an out of hospital respiratory arrest, and he has since relied on nocturnal ventilation via a tracheostomy tube. This patient has slowly progressive mild to moderate limb weakness but remains ambulant despite significant axial involvement. He also has left ventricular cardiac dysfunction. In addition, these images demonstrate bilateral non‐congenital elbow contractures and marked generalized muscle hypotrophy. (D) Family 24 female proband who was born following a pregnancy complicated by reduced fetal movements and breech presentation. She was significantly hypotonic at birth and had congenital bilateral wrist contractures and fixed foot deformities. (E, F) Ptosis and facial weakness in the younger sibling (Sibling 2) from Family 26 during early childhood (E) and at age 12 years (F). (G, H) The same child as shown in (A) at age 2 years with a pectus excavatum chest wall deformity (G) and scapular winging (H). (I) Distal joint hypermobility in Sibling 1 from Family 1. (J, K) Reduced range of neck movement in Sibling 1 from Family 26. (L–N) Lower limb MRI result for Sibling 1 from Family 26 at age 15 years. This shows fatty infiltration of the gluteal muscles (L), severe fatty replacement of both visible hamstring muscles (complete fatty replacement of semitendinosus and incomplete but marked replacement of biceps femoris; M), relative sparing of the adductor compartment, and mild involvement of the calf and peroneal muscles (N). (O–Q) Lower limb MRI result for the Family 31 proband at age 29 years. This is one of the 4 segregation‐inconclusive cases described in this paper, but not included within the clinical analysis cohort. The features in this individual's lower limb MRI may represent the more severe end of the spectrum of muscle involvement associated with this disorder. The images show severe fatty replacement of the quadriceps and hamstrings, sparing of the adductors (also seen in the first MRI case), and severe replacement of both calves and the right peroneus, with mild fatty marbling in the anterior and lateral compartment on the left. Consent was obtained from patients/parents/legal guardians for use of the clinical photographs shown in this figure, including the non‐obscured facial photographs shown in D–F.

Figure. Neuroimaging studies and sural nerve biopsy in CCPD associated with MOG-IgG1 antibodies (A) Gadolinium-enhanced axial T1-weighted MRI of the brain, showing several enhancing lesions in the right pons/middle cerebellar peduncle (trigeminal nerve entry zone), (C) the left periatral region and subcortical white matter of both cerebral hemispheres; and (B and D) at 3-month follow-up, showing resolution of the previously observed contrast-enhancing supratentorial and infratentorial lesions. Cutaneous lichen amyloidosis.caption: Figure 2 Clinical and histological presentation of MEN 2A-related CLA caused by theRETC611Y mutation. (A) Clinical characteristics of cutaneous lichen amyloidosis (CLA). Brown hyperpigmentation, scaly, dry and thickened papular skin with scabby scratches on the interscapular region (individual III:12). (B, C and D) Histopathological features of CLA. (D) The papillary dermal globular deposits of amyloid were positive for crystal violet staining (white asterisk) and overlying pigmentary incontinence (black arrow; original magnification, ×200).

Decreased cardiac output.caption: A 35-year-old man with decreased cardiac output undergoing CT scan demonstrates an axial non-contrast CT image showing an entrapped heart appearance with diffuse pericardial calcification, particularly involving the anterior and diaphragmatic aspects of the heart, which is consistent with constrictive pericarditis. The calcification pattern is widespread and may be associated with chronic inflammation, prior infection, or surgical history, as pericardial calcification can arise from various etiologies including tuberculosis, radiation, or idiopathic pericarditis.

Adipocyte hypertrophy.caption: Figure 1 Bimodal effects on adipogenesis in a manner of concentration dependence ofAntidesma buniusL. (MM) extract was displayed. Induction on pre-adipocyte differentiation and lipid accumulation at low concentrations (62.5–250 µg/mL) expressed by increasing of Oil Red O staining intensity which visualized the adipocyte hypertrophy and hyperplasia under inverted microscope (magnification 20×), in contrast, inhibitory effect at high concentration (500–1000 µg/mL) (A). In similar manner, the bimodal effects were demonstrated by total intracellular lipid accumulation and triglyceride content (B) with statistical significance (*) atp-value < 0.05 when compared to control (differentiated adipocytes).

Disseminated histoplasmosis.caption: Figure 1 Disseminated histoplasmosis and HIV infection; six characteristic images: (a) Pulmonary form; Chest CT-scan: diffuse micronodular opacities of the two lungs in a patient with severe form. (b) Lymphadenopathic form with IRIS; supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. (c) Direct examination (MGG stain) of bone marrow aspiration: parasitic form ofHistoplasma capsulatum; small budding yeasts (2–4 µm) surrounded by a pseudo-capsule, intra, and extra-macrophagic. (d) Digestive form; colonoscopy: colonic ulceration. (e) Cutaneous form; patient with disseminated papules. (f) Mucous form: patient with ulcerations of the oral mucosa.

Bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneu.caption: Figure 2 (a) Axial and (b) coronal computed tomography high resolution scans on admission showed mildly diminished lung volume with interstitial opacities, mainly in a basal and subpleural distribution, in the form of septal thickening, traction bronchiectasis, and small cystic changes matching with interstitial pulmonary fibrosis. Also noted was ground-glass veiling associated with patchy peripheral consolidation, which raises the suspicion of areas of bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneumonia. (c) Axial and (d) coronal scans three years after treatment showed relative regression of both ground-glass appearance and the patchy consolidation.

Small foramen magnum.caption: Axial CT images of the foramen magnum in a 28-year-old female with classical Chiari malformation type I, revealing an abnormally small foramen magnum with a significantly reduced transverse diameter (25.2 mm) and area (1,187.5 mm²), while the anterior–posterior diameter remains within normal limits, resulting in an exaggerated oval shape characteristic of this condition.

Axial CT images of the foramen magnum in a 17-year-old male with Chiari malformation type II, revealing an abnormally large foramen magnum with significantly increased anterior–posterior diameter (37.5 mm), transverse diameter (37.1 mm), and area (2,087.7 mm²), resulting in a rounded shape, a feature commonly seen in association with myelodysplasia and complex developmental anomalies.

Axial CT images of the foramen magnum in a 25-year-old female with Chiari malformation type I and craniosynostosis (Crouzon’s disease), demonstrating an abnormally small foramen magnum with a significantly reduced transverse diameter (24.8 mm) and area (1,185.4 mm²), and a normal anterior–posterior diameter, leading to an exaggerated oval shape, consistent with cranial constriction associated with syndromic craniosynostosis.

Axial CT images of the foramen magnum in a 27-year-old female with Chiari malformation type I and tethered cord syndrome, showing an abnormally large foramen magnum with significantly increased anterior–posterior diameter (40.5 mm), transverse diameter (35.1 mm), and area (2,084.7 mm²), resulting in a rounded shape, a finding associated with spinal cord tethering and altered cerebrospinal fluid dynamics.

Lateral ventricle dilatation.caption: Figure 1 Brain MRI studies of patients with XLCD. 1 a-c (male proband of a family with X-linked congenital ataxia) Midsagittal T1-weighted image showing an overall size reduction of the cerebellum predominant in the vermis with widened interfolia sulci. Brainstem is normal (a). Axial T2-weighted image showing global cerebellar hypoplasia without dysplasia (b). Axial T2-weighted image showing normal cortex and ventricles (c). d-f (male patient carrying aOPHN1mutation): midsagittal T1-weighted image showing hypoplastic cerebellar vermis and enlarged cisterna magna (d) axial T2-weighted image showing cerebellar hypoplasia and vermis dysplasia (e) axial T2-weighted image showing lateral ventricle dilatation and mild cortical atrophy (f). g-i (female patient carrying aCASK mutation): Midsagittal T1-weighted image showing moderate vermis hypoplasia and flat pons (g). Branchial fistula.caption: A 15-year-old male child with severe chronic kidney disease, acidosis, anemia, and metabolic bone disease, presenting with a preauricular tag on the right side, which is a small, skin-covered appendage located near the ear, consistent with a congenital anomaly commonly associated with branchial cleft abnormalities and other craniofacial malformations.

with no external urethral obstruction but significant vaginal distension due to hematocolpos, as seen in the clinical image.

Round ear.caption: Sub-figure i depicts a nevus of Ota, characterized by hypo and hyperpigmentary changes, starting medially near the root of the nose and progressing over the cheek, with involvement of the eyelid.

Sub-figure ii shows another nevus of Ota, presenting with hypo and hyperpigmentary changes, originating medially close to the root of the nose and spreading across the cheek, with involvement of the eyelid.

Cochlear aplasia.caption: Fig. 3 Inner ear dysplasias.a-dPatient 1: a 6-month-old girl presenting with congenital deafness has bilateral congenital malformations of the labyrinth on CT. On the right (a) axial CT shows a small cyst in place of the cochleovestibular system, corresponding tolabyrinthine aplasia. The internal auditory canal (IAC) is also absent. On the left, axial (b,c) and coronal (d) CT shows an IAC bending posteriorly towards a cystic dilated vestibular system. There is no development of the cochlea anteriorly; hence this developmental anomaly can be classified as acochlear aplasia.e,fPatient 2: on axial CT images of this deaf child, a widened cochlea without a modiolus renders a cystic appearance of the cochlea. The vestibule also shows pronounced enlargement. This has been classified as acystic cochleovestibular dysplasia (incomplete partition type I).g,hPatient 3 shows aclassic Mondini malformation (incomplete partition type II)consisting of a cochlea with a normal basal turn, but confluent middle and apical turn (black arrow), a widened vestibule (V) and an enlarged vestibular aqueduct (VA) (the normal size of the VA is 1–1.5 mm at its midpoint and generally does not exceed the size of the SCC). The modiolus is deficient (white arrowhead). On a 3D T2 TSE MR image the enlarged endolymphatic duct and sac are well seen (white arrows).i,jPatient 4: in this patient a foreshortened cochlea and a cystic appearance of the vestibular system is seen without enlargement of the vestibular aqueduct (black arrow). This dysplasia is more difficult to identify within the current classification system.k,lPatient 5: absence of the posterior semicircular canal illustrates that the abnormal development may be limited to the semicircular canals.Isolated absenceof the posteriorSCCmay be seen in Waardenburg or Allagile syndrome

Axonal loss.caption: Figure 8 Ultrastructure of optic nerve axonal loss.A: One year after injection of scAAV-GFP, transmission electron micrographs disclosed optic nerve axons (a) of various sizes enveloped by myelin sheaths. Astroglial cell processes (g) coursed between fibers of the optic nerve.B: In this same animal, the opposite eye injected with the MTS scAAV mutantND4had a marked decrease in axonal density. Many empty spaces (e) were present where axons were apparently lost in these atrophic optic nerves and degenerating axonal profiles were evident (d).C: In a different animal, the optic nerve of the control eye injected with scAAV-GFPhad normal axons (a) with the only empty space the lumen of a blood vessel (L).D: In this animal, the opposite eye injected with the MTS scAAV mutant ND4 had a marked decrease in axonal density. Many empty spaces (e) were present where axons were lost. a=optic nerve axon, e=empty space, g=astroglial process, d=degenerating axon, L=lumen.

EBV encephalitis.caption: MRI of the CNS in a patient with herpesvirus-associated CNS disease, specifically HSV-1 encephalitis, shows bilateral and symmetric hyperintense lesions involving the frontal and temporal lobes, insula, and splenium of the corpus callosum on FLAIR imaging, indicative of acute inflammatory involvement of these regions.

MRI of the CNS in a patient with herpesvirus-associated CNS disease, specifically EBV encephalitis, demonstrates large patchy areas of increased signal intensity in the bilateral white matter under the cortex on FLAIR imaging, suggesting focal inflammatory or demyelinating changes in the subcortical white matter.

Moderate myopia.caption: Typical retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) defects observed in eyes with emmetropia, as seen in a 50-year-old female patient with −0.25 diopters of spherical equivalent. The image shows the right eye, revealing characteristic RNFL abnormalities associated with emmetropia, with angle I measuring 40.89°, angle II measuring 15.69°, and a mean deviation (MD) of -2.97.

Typical retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) defects observed in eyes with low to moderate myopia, as seen in a 37-year-old female patient with −2.00 diopters of spherical equivalent. The image shows the left eye, revealing characteristic RNFL abnormalities associated with low to moderate myopia, with angle I measuring 37.85°, angle II measuring 25.87°, and a mean deviation (MD) of -1.09.

Figure 6 Gross findings in rams experimentally infected byHistophilus somni. ((a) and (b)) Unilateral epididymitis associated with testicular atrophy. (a) The body and tail of left epididymis are severally increased in size with tunica vaginalis adhered and ipsilateral testis strongly decreased. (b) Hematoma in tunica vaginalis adjacent to the tail of the epididymis. (c) Epididymal abscess. Body of left epididymis is increased in size and focal area in cut surface with yellowish viscous material. (d) Chronic periorchitis. Multifocal fibrous adhesions between the tunica albuginea and the tunica vaginalis.

Multiple cutaneous leiomyomas.caption: Computed tomography (CT) scan of the pelvis in a female HLRCC patient, revealing multiple large uterine leiomyomas (white arrows), which are commonly found in women with HLRCC and can be symptomatic, often requiring surgical intervention such as myomectomy or hysterectomy before the age of 40.

A 45-year-old female with hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell carcinoma (HLRCC) presenting with multiple cutaneous leiomyomas on the torso, appearing as firm, flesh-colored nodules ranging from 0.4 to 2.5 mm in size, often symptomatic with pain and paresthesias, and typically developing by the age of 25, with a range of 10–47 years.

Smooth tongue.caption: a smooth tongue with complete loss of the normal filiform and fungiform papillae, revealing a characteristic appearance of a smooth, glossy tongue surface

Glomerular capillary collapse.caption: Figure 2 Light microscopy again showing collapsing focal segmental glomerulosclerosis with mesangial hypercellularity and glomerular capillary collapse.

Blake's pouch cyst.caption: Coronal T2-weighted MR image of a 5-month-old patient with tetraventricular hydrocephalus and Blake's pouch cyst (BPC) demonstrating severe hydrocephalus with features consistent with BPC, including tetraventricular dilation, a relatively well-developed nonrotated cerebellar vermis, and compression of the medial cerebellar hemispheres.

Sagittal T2-weighted MR image of a 5-month-old patient with tetraventricular hydrocephalus and Blake's pouch cyst (BPC) demonstrating severe hydrocephalus with bulging third ventricular floor, open aqueduct, and dilated fourth ventricle. The cerebellar vermis is compressed but relatively well-developed and nonrotated, consistent with BPC. A thin line between the dilated fourth ventricle and cisterna magna (black arrow) indicates a Blake's pouch cyst, and a high torcular Herophili with upward displacement of the tentorium is observed.

Prominent eyelashes.caption: A 3-year-old patient with Kabuki syndrome, presenting with characteristic facial features including arched eyebrows, epicanthus, ptosis, prominent eyelashes, long palpebral fissures, eversion of the lateral portion of the lower eyelids in both eyes, and a depressed nasal tip, as seen in the external photograph.
